title: Untitled
research_problem: The paper studies pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard near Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. The goal is to understand pedestrian crossing compliance, preferences, and factors influencing crossing decisions at different types of facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, and various treatments like physical barriers, shelters, and colored paving.
method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) pedestrian user survey with 711 respondents analyzing crossing patterns, preferences, and perceptions; (2) video-camera observation of pedestrian movements using 8 cameras at a 1-km study site on Grand River Avenue. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates were calculated from both data sources and compared.
datasets: Pedestrian survey (n=711) - daily and occasional users of Grand River Avenue between Abbott and Bogue Streets; Video observation data from 8 strategically placed cameras capturing pedestrian movements during peak hours (10:30-1:00 PM and 2:30-6:00 PM) on weekdays
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using crosswalks at designated locations; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage of pedestrians complying with WALK signal indication; Survey response percentages for preference and perception questions
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian crossing facility with 83% of respondents preferring to cross at these locations and showing a high crossing compliance rate of 71.2%
limitations: Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue) near a university campus, limiting generalizability to other urban environments | Survey sample predominantly consists of university affiliates (students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population | Video observation method may have some double-counting issues despite mitigation efforts, and weather conditions were limited (no rainy day data) | Self-reported survey data may not fully capture actual pedestrian behavior due to social desirability bias
notes: Study funded by Michigan Department of Transportation. The study site was renovated in 1994-1995 to include midblock crosswalks, pedestrian warning signs, physical barriers, and designated crossing locations. Key finding: midblock crosswalks are most effective for compliance while signalized crosswalks are best for pedestrian channelization but have lower signal compliance. Age was found to significantly affect response about distance as a factor (elderly pedestrians less concerned about distance than younger groups).

title: Untitled
research_problem: The research addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) is highly prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% at certain sections). Existing micro-simulation tools developed in foreign countries cannot accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and high violation rates.
method_summary: The paper develops a pedestrian behavior model for micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Questionnaires administered to 495 respondents in Xi'an were used to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances (policeman presence, traffic volume, weather, other pedestrians' behavior). Time gap (ΔT) distribution was extracted from videotape (989 samples) using image-processing software to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model calculates safety gaps based on pedestrian walking speed (1.2 m/s), vehicle distance, and vehicle speed. Parameters were calibrated and simulation results validated against field data extracted from videotape.
method_family: Microscopic simulation model / Behavioral model with gap acceptance
tasks: Pedestrian signal non-compliance decision modeling; Gap acceptance behavior modeling; Pedestrian crossing simulation at signalized intersections
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of time gaps)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time gap (ΔT) distribution; Pedestrian arrival interval distribution
claim_text: The developed model accurately represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results matching field observations after parameter adjustment (red light runner proportion: 0.82 mean vs 0.85 field; violation follower proportion: 0.19 mean vs 0.18 field)
limitations: The model only considers pedestrians' decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration) during the crossing process | The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical for simulating Chinese city traffic | Questionnaire results may not fully represent all pedestrians, as respondents were willing participants and answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data | The model was only validated in Xi'an and may not generalize to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
notes: The model addresses a significant gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries like China. Key innovation is the classification of pedestrians into two types with dynamic decision-making influenced by external factors. The 55% proportion of law-abiding pedestrians (Type I) from questionnaire had to be adjusted to 35% to better match field observations. The study used Visual C++ 6.0 for simulation and M3 distribution for vehicle headways. The paper has been cited 162 times, indicating significant research impact.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models for signalized crosswalks fail to capture key behaviors including group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary
method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model integrates desired direction modeling using Weibull distribution, stop/go decision modeling using binary Logit for pedestrian flashing green phase, and stochastic giving-way maneuver for collision avoidance. Parameters are calibrated in two stages: measurable parameters directly estimated from observed trajectory data, and non-measurable parameters derived by Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
datasets: Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan (north crosswalk) - 1352 pedestrians, 124 left-turning vehicles, 97,200 position samples
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%; Two-sample t-test for passing positions at cross-sections (95% confidence); Number of formed lanes comparison; Passing time distribution; Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction
claim_text: The modified social force model achieves high accuracy in pedestrian trajectory prediction with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (estimated peak shift 2.5m vs observed 2m) | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship as influencing factors | The visual range parameters (angle 170°, radius 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations
notes: This study addresses the gap in pedestrian behavior modeling at signalized crosswalks by extending the classical social force model. Key innovations include modeling group vs individual evasive behavior, giving-way maneuver with stochastic adjustment mechanism, elliptical force field for left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phases. The model is calibrated using video-derived trajectory data from a Japanese intersection and validated through multiple performance metrics including trajectory comparison, passing position distribution, collision avoidance behavior, and self-organization phenomena.

Key innovations include modeling group vs individual evasive behavior, giving-way maneuver with stochastic adjustment mechanism, elliptical force field for left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phases. The model is calibrated using video-derived trajectory data from a Japanese intersection and validated through multiple performance metrics including trajectory comparison, passing position distribution, collision avoidance behavior, and self-organization phenomena.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models for signalized crosswalks fail to capture key behaviors including group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary
method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model integrates desired direction modeling using Weibull distribution, stop/go decision modeling using binary Logit for pedestrian flashing green phase, and stochastic giving-way maneuver for collision avoidance. Parameters are calibrated in two stages: measurable parameters directly estimated from observed trajectory data, and non-measurable parameters derived by Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
datasets: Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan (north crosswalk) - 1352 pedestrians, 124 left-turning vehicles, 97,200 position samples
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%; Two-sample t-test for passing positions at cross-sections (95% confidence); Number of formed lanes comparison; Passing time distribution; Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction
claim_text: The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force has the largest value among calibrated parameters, indicating pedestrian collision avoidance with vehicles is a dominant factor in crossing behavior at signalized crosswalks
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (estimated peak shift 2.5m vs observed 2m) | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship as influencing factors | The visual range parameters (angle 170°, radius 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations

title: Untitled
research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where signal non-compliance (red light running) is prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% in certain sections), requiring a micro-simulation model that captures the high rate of pedestrian violations and mixed traffic characteristics unique to developing Chinese cities
method_summary: Pedestrian micro-simulation model classifying pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Model parameters calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions under different circumstances, and time gap (DT) distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria for pedestrians deciding to cross during conflicts with vehicles
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents (200 students + 300 random pedestrians) at Xi'an Jiaotong University intersection, November 2003; Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT calculations)
metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; distribution_of_pedestrian_arriving_intervals; DT_time_gap_acceptance_ratio
claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
limitations: Model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration) | DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to parameter deviation | Pedestrians' estimation of vehicle velocity cannot be directly extracted from videotape, requiring use of instantaneous speed as approximation
notes: Model framework uses three proportion parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians, ~55% baseline), r2 (proportion of Type I pedestrians who follow others to violate, adjusted to 35%), and r3 (proportion accepting specific DT gap). Pedestrian crossing uses one-by-one mode considering time gaps in adjacent lane only. Average walking speed assumed as 1.2 m/s. Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China influenced violation rates.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the theory of planned behavior (TPB) with new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
method_summary: Used structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. Designed a questionnaire with a scenario about crossing against pedestrian signals, measuring constructs including instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. Four models were compared using goodness-of-fit indices (CMIN/DF, RMSEA, CFI).
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intention; Evaluating contributions of TPB components and extended factors
datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (260 complete and valid responses)
metrics: CMIN/DF ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOSE; CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Cronbach's alpha for reliability; Regression weights
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models (p<0.001 for all models)
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain high internal reliability could affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other populations or cultures | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the crossing scenario was not challenging enough for the sample population
notes: The paper extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency; Descriptive norm (observation of others' behavior) was found more influential than subjective norm (perceived approval); High conformity tendency pedestrians more likely to violate when seeing others do so; Perceived risk was not significant, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive danger of violations; Findings have implications for safety interventions targeting attitude change, social norm correction, and safety awareness campaigns

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection in Hefei, China. Used two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to measure visual attention. Four conditions tested: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA used for statistical analysis.
tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under four distraction conditions; Music distraction: listening to music via headphones during red light countdown and crossing; Phone conversation distraction: answering mental arithmetic questions while crossing; Text distraction: replying to mental arithmetic text messages while crossing
datasets: 28 college students (17 male, 11 female), ages 17-25, recruited from Hefei University of Technology; Single signalized intersection with 14-meter wide crosswalk, 21-second green light phase, 40-km/h speed limit; Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017
metrics: Crossing behavior: initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Crossing behavior: look left/right frequency (attention to traffic); Crossing behavior: crossing speed (mean walking speed); Visual attention: pupil diameter (cognitive load); Visual attention: scanning frequency (visual alertness); Visual attention: fixation points on 5 AOIs (phone, crosswalk, traffic signal, right traffic, left traffic); Visual attention: fixation time on 5 AOIs; Visual attention: mean fixation duration on 5 AOIs
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance compared to phone conversation and music distractions
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) and homogeneous sample (college students only) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect how pedestrians use mobile phones in daily life | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), not representative of complex traffic environments | Experimental conditions may not conform exactly to participants' daily life - unfamiliar phone used and mental arithmetic may not represent typical phone tasks | Study only examined three specific mobile phone tasks; other activities (mobile internet, social media) not studied | Eye movement data complement video data but both methods have inherent limitations in measuring attention allocation accurately
notes: Key finding: text distraction interferes most with pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation, then music. All three distractions impair visual attention allocation to traffic environment compared to undistracted condition. The study uses Tobii Pro Glasses 2 for eye tracking in outdoor environment - a methodological strength over previous virtual-reality or observational studies. Findings support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations for distracted pedestrians.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running (RLR) violation propensity at signalized urban intersections, including personal factors (gender, age, companion presence) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other violators)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory variables, including both individual-level and cycle-level factors. Both models with and without interaction effects were compared using AIC.
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; 6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each had >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; odds_ratios; marginal_effects_elasticities; AIC_Akaike_Information_Criterion; z_values_for_statistical_significance
claim_text: Pedestrian gender, age, companion presence, number of pedestrians around, presence of other violators in same cycle, time to green, red time, traffic volume, and percentage of heavy vehicles all significantly affect red light running propensity
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity rather than actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured | Study conducted only in Hong Kong; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic rules, pedestrian signal systems, or cultural factors | Observation survey cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitudes, or detailed socioeconomic characteristics directly
notes: Study uniquely combines individual-level (pedestrian demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors. Considers social influence effects from presence and behaviors of other pedestrians. Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior performance (AIC 2750.1 vs 2776.6). title: Untitled
research_problem: Exploration of factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, with the goal of understanding why pedestrians run red-lights and identifying which pedestrian groups and intersections are more likely to have such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing facilities. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection and iterative calculation for parameter estimation.
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (collected May-June 2015)
metrics: score_test; odds_ratio; statistical_significance
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs. work/school), time period in a day (daytime, dusk, night vs. dawn), pedestrian's attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards whether quality of road facility affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not be generalizable to other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors | Sample size of 631, while sufficient for modeling, could be expanded with more data from multiple intersections and cities to validate findings | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running at signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings |  | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects | Methodology may have self-selection bias as survey relied on self-reported behavior rather than observed behavior
notes: Paper published in Accident Analysis and Prevention 96 (2016) 71-78; Survey conducted in Hefei, Anhui Province, China during May-June 2015; Variables considered include: gender, age, education, profession, income, trip purpose, time requirement, intersection familiarity, tolerable waiting time, time segment, and five attitude variables; 15 explanatory variables were initially considered; 13 were significant at 95% confidence level in Score test; final model retained 4 significant variables

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) at unsignalized midblock crosswalks and its effect on traffic flow
method_summary: Developed a microscopic traffic flow model integrating car-following theory with probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance. The model extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model to incorporate VY and PGA behaviors. Vehicles decide whether to yield based on pedestrian crossing status and logistic probability; pedestrians decide whether to cross based on gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and location. Time-step simulation in MATLAB was used with parameters from empirical data collected in Shanghai, China.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, car-following model (FVD extension), discrete-time simulation
tasks: Model interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance; Analyze traffic flow characteristics at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Evaluate effects of traffic volume, pedestrian volume, and number of lanes; Recommend control mode (signalized vs unsignalized) based on operational performance
datasets: Empirical data from 13 unsignalized midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (Zhao et al., 2019); Validation data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h); Validation data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
metrics: Average vehicular delay (s); Average pedestrian waiting time (s); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values; Wilcoxon signed-rank test p-value for model validation
claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes vehicle-pedestrian interaction at unsignalized midblock crosswalks with relative error less than 10%
limitations: Model only considers legal pedestrian crossing behavior at designated crosswalks, not illegal crossing | Model calibrated with data from Shanghai, China only; generalizability to other regions uncertain | Does not account for driver-pedestrian nonverbal communication (eye contact, gestures) | Stochastic behavior modeled via logit probabilities may not fully capture real-world decision complexity
notes: Paper published in Transportation Research Part F (2020); Uses car-following theory as foundation with VY and PGA logit models integrated; Maximum relative error of 8% between model and field observations; Model parameters: sensitivity parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, optimal velocity parameters V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, vehicle length=9m; Recommended analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) for stable results based on stability analysis

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior under mixed traffic conditions at signalized intersections, including crossing speed variations, compliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions
method_summary: Video recording surveys conducted at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours; statistical analysis including Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, and Student t tests; logistic regression modeling for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
metrics: crossing_speed_m_s; compliance_rate; pedestrian-vehicular_interaction_frequency; CSDF_crossing_speed_deviation_factor
claim_text: The average pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections in Mumbai is 1.34 m/s, which exceeds the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s
limitations: Study conducted only in Mumbai, India with 3 intersections; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns | Logistic regression models could benefit from larger sample sizes for more robust statistical results
notes: Paper introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric for understanding pedestrian speed variations; logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction prediction; study addresses gap in literature regarding pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections in mixed traffic conditions

datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
metrics: crossing_speed_m_s; compliance_rate; pedestrian-vehicular_interaction_frequency; CSDF_crossing_speed_deviation_factor
claim_text: Design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians are recommended based on analysis
limitations: Study conducted only in Mumbai, India with 3 intersections; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns | Logistic regression models could benefit from larger sample sizes for more robust statistical results
notes: Paper introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric for understanding pedestrian speed variations; logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction prediction; study addresses gap in literature regarding pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections in mixed traffic conditions

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions and behavior at signalized intersections for collision avoidance in connected vehicles, specifically addressing the hazardous scenario of pedestrian-vehicle collisions at turning locations.
method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context (traffic signal phases), pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. The model uses particle filtering for state estimation including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan using three monocular cameras. 289 pedestrian sequences total: 251 cross-scenarios and 38 wait-scenarios. Pedestrian grounding points, motion types, crosswalk edge positions, and traffic signal phases were manually labeled.
metrics: confusion_matrices_for_decision_and_motion_recognition; positioning_error_mean_and_std; recognition_accuracy_at_different_time_from_decision_moments
claim_text: The DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels
limitations: The model only handles left-turn scenarios in Japan (left-hand traffic), limiting generalizability to other traffic contexts | The dataset is imbalanced with only 38 wait-pedestrians compared to 251 cross-pedestrians, potentially affecting wait decision recognition | Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions (Table 4 shows running precision of only 35-56%) | The model assumes straight trajectories and cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories well (positioning errors larger in cross-scenarios with curving) | Large observation errors cause recognition delay and decreased accuracy in decision and motion classification
notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at signalized intersections in Japan where pedestrian signals have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red). The model assumes pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG. The research is motivated by the fact that nearly half of pedestrian-vehicle accidents in Japan occur at intersections. The approach uses V2I communication assumptions for obtaining traffic signal information.
